sections.title: If—
sections.summary: This poem gives a fatherly lesson about becoming mature, steady, disciplined, humble, brave, patient, and emotionally balanced. It describes the qualities a person needs to handle pressure, success, failure, lies, hate, loss, power, people, and time without losing character.
sections.mainIdea: True maturity means controlling yourself under pressure, staying honest when others are dishonest, treating success and failure calmly, beginning again after loss, keeping humility with all kinds of people, and using every moment fully.
sections.modernTakeaway: A strong person is not someone who never suffers. A strong person keeps their judgment, values, effort, and humility even when life becomes unfair, uncertain, painful, or tempting.

lines.original: If you can keep your head when all about you
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can stay calm and think clearly when everyone around you is panicking or acting emotionally.

difficultWords.definition: Stay calm, sensible, and in control of your thinking.
Everyone around you.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that maturity begins with self-control under pressure.
lines.modernExample: In a group project, everyone starts blaming each other before the deadline, but you stay calm, organize the work, and focus on solving the problem.
lines.connection: This is the opening condition of the poem, so it starts the whole lesson by focusing on calmness.
lines.application: When people around you panic, do not copy their panic. Pause, breathe, understand the facts, and decide your next useful action.
lines.checkQuestion: What does it mean to keep your head in a stressful situation?

lines.original: Are losing theirs and blaming it on you,
lines.simpleEnglish: When other people are losing control and saying that you are the reason for the problem.

difficultWords.definition: Losing their calmness, judgment, or self-control.
Saying someone is responsible for something bad.

lines.proof: The writer is showing that real calmness is tested when others unfairly accuse you, not only when things are easy.
lines.modernExample: A customer shouts at you for a mistake caused by the system, but you do not shout back; you calmly help fix the issue.
lines.connection: This completes the thought from the previous line: you must keep calm while others lose calm and blame you.
lines.application: When accused unfairly, separate two things: what actually happened and how you should respond with dignity.
lines.checkQuestion: Why is it harder to stay calm when people blame you?

lines.original: If you can trust yourself when all men doubt you,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can believe in your own judgment even when everyone else doubts you.

difficultWords.definition: Have confidence in your own judgment and ability.
To be unsure or suspicious about something.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that a mature person does not lose all confidence just because others do not believe in him.
lines.modernExample: You choose a career path others do not understand, but you have studied it carefully and continue with confidence.
lines.connection: After teaching calmness under blame, the poem now teaches confidence under doubt.
lines.application: When people doubt you, check your reasons. If your reasons are strong, continue without needing everyone’s approval.
lines.checkQuestion: What should you do when others doubt you but your reasons are strong?

lines.original: But make allowance for their doubting too;
lines.simpleEnglish: But also understand that other people may have reasons for doubting you, so do not become arrogant.

difficultWords.definition: Accept or consider something fairly.
Questioning whether something is true, right, or reliable.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that true confidence is balanced with humility. You trust yourself, but you still respect the possibility that others may see something you missed.
lines.modernExample: You believe your business idea is good, but when experienced people raise concerns, you listen carefully instead of dismissing them.
lines.connection: This balances the previous line. The previous line says trust yourself; this line says do not become blind or arrogant while doing so.
lines.application: Use other people’s doubt as feedback, not as a prison. Listen, examine, adjust if needed, but do not collapse emotionally.
lines.checkQuestion: How can you trust yourself without becoming arrogant?

lines.original: If you can wait and not be tired by waiting,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can be patient and not become frustrated just because success or results take time.

difficultWords.definition: Stay patient until something happens.
Mentally exhausted, irritated, or discouraged because something is taking long.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that maturity requires patience, not just action and ambition.
lines.modernExample: You study for months for an exam and do not quit just because improvement is slow.
lines.connection: After confidence and humility, the poem adds patience as another quality needed for strength.
lines.application: When progress is slow, focus on the daily process instead of demanding immediate results.
lines.checkQuestion: Why is patience important for long-term success?

lines.original: Or being lied about, don’t deal in lies,
lines.simpleEnglish: If people tell lies about you, do not respond by lying yourself.

difficultWords.definition: When false things are said about someone.
Use lies or dishonest methods.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that your character should not be controlled by other people’s bad behavior.
lines.modernExample: Someone spreads a false rumor about you at work, but you answer with facts instead of spreading rumors about them.
lines.connection: This continues the patience idea: while waiting and suffering unfairness, you must still stay honest.
lines.application: Do not let dishonest people turn you into a dishonest person. Correct the lie if needed, but keep your own integrity.
lines.checkQuestion: What should you avoid doing when someone lies about you?

lines.original: Or being hated, don’t give way to hating,
lines.simpleEnglish: If people hate you, do not let yourself become hateful in return.

difficultWords.definition: Strongly disliked or treated with hostility.
Allow yourself to be controlled by something.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that strength means refusing to copy the ugliness directed at you.
lines.modernExample: People insult you online, but instead of becoming cruel, you either respond calmly or disengage.
lines.connection: This matches the previous line: do not answer lies with lies, and do not answer hate with hate.
lines.application: When someone attacks you emotionally, choose your response based on your values, not their behavior.
lines.checkQuestion: What does the poem say you should not do when others hate you?

lines.original: And yet don’t look too good, nor talk too wise:
lines.simpleEnglish: But even while acting well, do not show off or act morally superior to others.

difficultWords.definition: Act as if you are better than everyone else.
Speak as if you are extremely clever or superior.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that virtue must be joined with humility. Being good should not become a performance of superiority.
lines.modernExample: You stay honest in a corrupt workplace, but you do not constantly lecture everyone about how moral you are.
lines.connection: This balances the previous lines. You must avoid lies and hate, but you must also avoid pride about being better.
lines.application: Do the right thing quietly. Let your behavior show your character more than your speeches.
lines.checkQuestion: Why should a good person avoid acting superior?

lines.original: If you can dream—and not make dreams your master;
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can have big dreams but not let dreams control your whole life or make you ignore reality.

difficultWords.definition: Imagine a future goal, hope, or possibility.
Something that controls you.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that ambition is good only when it remains under self-control.
lines.modernExample: You dream of becoming famous, but you do not sacrifice your health, family, honesty, or peace just to chase fame.
lines.connection: After discussing self-control with other people, the poem now turns to self-control over inner ambitions.
lines.application: Set goals, but do not worship them. A dream should guide you, not enslave you.
lines.checkQuestion: What is the danger of making dreams your master?

lines.original: If you can think—and not make thoughts your aim;
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can think deeply but not stop at thinking alone; you must also act.

difficultWords.definition: The main goal or target.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that thought is useful, but life cannot be only planning, analyzing, or imagining.
lines.modernExample: You read many books about fitness, but if you never exercise, your thinking has not become action.
lines.connection: This repeats the balance from the previous line: dream, but do not be ruled by dreams; think, but do not live only in thoughts.
lines.application: Use thinking to guide action. Do not let overthinking replace practice.
lines.checkQuestion: Why is thinking not enough by itself?

lines.original: If you can meet with Triumph and Disaster
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can face both great success and great failure.

difficultWords.definition: Great success or victory.
Great failure, loss, or trouble.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that life will bring both victory and failure, and maturity requires meeting both steadily.
lines.modernExample: One month your business earns a lot of money; the next month it suffers a big loss. You do not become arrogant in success or destroyed by failure.
lines.connection: After dreams and thoughts, the poem moves to real outcomes: success and failure.
lines.application: When success or failure arrives, ask how to respond wisely instead of letting the event define your worth.
lines.checkQuestion: What two opposite experiences does this line mention?

lines.original: And treat those two impostors just the same;
lines.simpleEnglish: And see success and failure as temporary appearances that should not control your identity.

difficultWords.definition: Things that pretend to be more important or final than they really are.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that both success and failure can deceive you. Success can make you proud, and failure can make you hopeless.
lines.modernExample: You get praised for one video going viral, but you do not think you are a genius. Another video fails, but you do not think you are worthless.
lines.connection: This completes the previous line by explaining how to treat Triumph and Disaster.
lines.application: Do not let success inflate you or failure crush you. Treat both as information, not as your identity.
lines.checkQuestion: Why does the poem call Triumph and Disaster impostors?

lines.original: If you can bear to hear the truth you’ve spoken
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can handle hearing your honest words repeated back to you.

difficultWords.definition: Endure or tolerate something difficult.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that even truth can become painful when other people misuse it.
lines.modernExample: You give honest advice in a meeting, and later someone quotes part of it out of context to make you look bad.
lines.connection: After success and failure, the poem now discusses another test: seeing your own truthful words used against you.
lines.application: Speak truth carefully, and be ready that dishonest people may twist it. Do not stop being truthful because of that risk.
lines.checkQuestion: What difficult thing must you be able to bear in this line?

lines.original: Twisted by knaves to make a trap for fools,
lines.simpleEnglish: When dishonest people distort your words to trick foolish people.

difficultWords.definition: Changed or distorted from the original meaning.
Dishonest or wicked people.
A setup meant to catch, harm, or trick someone.
People who are easily misled or lack good judgment.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that moral strength includes surviving manipulation and misrepresentation.
lines.modernExample: A politician, influencer, or coworker cuts your words into a misleading clip so others misunderstand you.
lines.connection: This explains what happens to the truth in the previous line: it gets twisted by dishonest people.
lines.application: When your words are misused, respond with clarity and patience, not panic or revenge.
lines.checkQuestion: Who twists the truth in this line?

lines.original: Or watch the things you gave your life to, broken,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can see something you worked extremely hard for become destroyed or fail.

difficultWords.definition: Spent enormous time, effort, and commitment on something.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that maturity is tested when your deepest work is damaged or lost.
lines.modernExample: You spend years building a business, project, relationship, or skill, and then it collapses because of circumstances or mistakes.
lines.connection: This continues the theme of suffering unfair or painful losses after your words and work are attacked.
lines.application: Do not make your identity depend completely on one project. If it breaks, grieve if needed, then decide what can be rebuilt.
lines.checkQuestion: What kind of loss is described in this line?

lines.original: And stoop and build ’em up with worn-out tools:
lines.simpleEnglish: And if you can humbly start rebuilding those broken things, even with weak resources and tired energy.

difficultWords.definition: Bend down; here it also suggests humility and willingness to do hard work.
Build them up; repair or recreate them.
Old, damaged, weak, or exhausted resources.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that real strength is not just surviving loss, but rebuilding after loss with whatever remains.
lines.modernExample: After losing your savings in a failed business, you begin again using your remaining skills, old contacts, and limited money.
lines.connection: This completes the previous line. First your work is broken; now you must rebuild it.
lines.application: When life breaks something important, ask: what tools, skills, people, or knowledge do I still have? Start there.
lines.checkQuestion: What does the poem ask you to do after your work is broken?

lines.original: If you can make one heap of all your winnings
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can gather everything you have gained into one pile.

difficultWords.definition: A pile or collection of things.
Things you have gained, earned, or won.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that courage sometimes requires being willing to risk what you have gained.
lines.modernExample: You save money for years and then invest it into starting your own company.
lines.connection: After rebuilding from broken work, the poem moves to risk: what if you put your gains on the line?
lines.application: Before a major risk, clearly know what you are risking and why. Courage is not blind; it is aware of the cost.
lines.checkQuestion: What does 'one heap of all your winnings' mean?

lines.original: And risk it on one turn of pitch-and-toss,
lines.simpleEnglish: And risk everything on one uncertain chance, like a gambling game.

difficultWords.definition: Put something valuable in danger for a possible gain.
An old coin-tossing gambling game based on chance.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that life sometimes demands bold risk, but the deeper test is how you handle the result.
lines.modernExample: You leave a safe job to build something meaningful, knowing it could fail.
lines.connection: This explains what happens to the heap from the previous line: it is risked on one chance.
lines.application: When taking a risk, prepare your mind for both outcomes so the result does not destroy your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What does 'pitch-and-toss' suggest about the risk?

lines.original: And lose, and start again at your beginnings
lines.simpleEnglish: And if you lose everything, you can begin again from the start.

difficultWords.definition: The starting point; the early stage before success.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that resilience means being able to restart after total loss.
lines.modernExample: Your startup fails, you lose your investment, and you return to learning, working, saving, and building again from zero.
lines.connection: This shows the negative result of the risk in the previous line: you lose, but you do not stop living or trying.
lines.application: Treat failure as a return to training, not as the end of your story.
lines.checkQuestion: What should you do after losing everything, according to this line?

lines.original: And never breathe a word about your loss;
lines.simpleEnglish: And you do not complain publicly or keep talking about how much you lost.

difficultWords.definition: Do not say anything about it.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that dignity means not turning loss into constant complaint or self-pity.
lines.modernExample: After a failed investment, you do not spend every conversation blaming luck or asking for sympathy. You quietly rebuild.
lines.connection: This adds emotional discipline to the previous line. You start again, and you do it without making your loss your identity.
lines.application: Share pain wisely when needed, but do not build your personality around complaint.
lines.checkQuestion: What does this line say you should avoid after a big loss?

lines.original: If you can force your heart and nerve and sinew
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can push your emotions, courage, and body to keep working.

difficultWords.definition: Here it means courage, feeling, and emotional strength.
Courage and mental toughness.
Strong body tissue; here it means physical strength.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that endurance requires the whole person: emotion, courage, and body.
lines.modernExample: During exam preparation, your mind is tired, your body is exhausted, and your confidence is low, but you still complete the day’s study.
lines.connection: After financial or life loss, the poem now talks about physical and mental endurance.
lines.application: When motivation disappears, use discipline to make your body and mind serve the task for a little longer.
lines.checkQuestion: What parts of yourself must you force to keep going in this line?

lines.original: To serve your turn long after they are gone,
lines.simpleEnglish: To keep helping you even after your normal energy, strength, and motivation feel finished.

difficultWords.definition: Help you do what is needed at that moment.
Used up, exhausted, or no longer easily available.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that strong people can continue after ordinary strength feels used up.
lines.modernExample: A nurse, athlete, student, or parent keeps going during a crisis even when they feel completely drained.
lines.connection: This completes the previous line by saying what the heart, nerve, and sinew must do: keep serving after exhaustion.
lines.application: Build habits before crisis so that when energy is gone, habit and duty can still carry you.
lines.checkQuestion: What does it mean to keep serving after your strength is gone?

lines.original: And so hold on when there is nothing in you
lines.simpleEnglish: And continue holding on even when you feel empty inside.

difficultWords.definition: Continue, endure, or refuse to give up.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that deep endurance appears when comfort, excitement, and easy motivation are gone.
lines.modernExample: You are depressed, tired, or discouraged, but you still get up, do the minimum necessary work, and refuse to quit completely.
lines.connection: This continues the endurance described in the previous two lines, going even deeper into exhaustion.
lines.application: In very hard moments, reduce the task to the next small action and hold on through that.
lines.checkQuestion: What does the poem say you should do when you feel empty inside?

lines.original: Except the Will which says to them: ‘Hold on!’
lines.simpleEnglish: The only thing left is your willpower, telling your tired body and mind to continue.

difficultWords.definition: The inner power to choose, continue, and direct yourself.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that when everything else is exhausted, the will can still command endurance.
lines.modernExample: A marathon runner’s legs are burning, but the mind keeps saying, 'One more step.'
lines.connection: This explains what remains when there is 'nothing in you' from the previous line: the Will remains.
lines.application: Train your will in small discomforts so it becomes available in major hardship.
lines.checkQuestion: What is the last inner power that tells you to continue?

lines.original: If you can talk with crowds and keep your virtue,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can be among ordinary people or large groups without losing your moral character.

difficultWords.definition: Large groups of people; here it can mean common public opinion.
Good character, moral strength, and right behavior.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that social pressure should not make you abandon your values.
lines.modernExample: All your friends are mocking someone, but you do not join in just to fit in.
lines.connection: After inner endurance, the poem now turns to social life and how to behave with people.
lines.application: When you are in a crowd, keep your own judgment instead of automatically copying the group.
lines.checkQuestion: What must you keep when talking with crowds?

lines.original: Or walk with Kings—nor lose the common touch,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can spend time with powerful or important people without becoming proud or disconnected from ordinary people.

difficultWords.definition: Be around powerful, rich, famous, or high-status people.
The ability to relate naturally and respectfully to ordinary people.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that a mature person can handle high status without becoming arrogant.
lines.modernExample: You meet CEOs, politicians, or celebrities, but you still treat workers, drivers, cleaners, and students with respect.
lines.connection: This balances the previous line: you must be steady with crowds and also humble with kings.
lines.application: Do not let the status of the people around you change your basic respect for everyone.
lines.checkQuestion: What does it mean to not lose the common touch?

lines.original: If neither foes nor loving friends can hurt you,
lines.simpleEnglish: If neither enemies nor close friends can control your inner peace or destroy you emotionally.

difficultWords.definition: Enemies or opponents.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that emotional independence requires not being ruled by hatred from enemies or attachment to friends.
lines.modernExample: Criticism from an enemy does not break you, and approval or disappointment from a friend does not control your whole life.
lines.connection: After talking about crowds and kings, the poem now includes both hostile and loving relationships.
lines.application: Care about people, but do not give any person total power over your self-worth.
lines.checkQuestion: Which two kinds of people should not be able to destroy your inner balance?

lines.original: If all men count with you, but none too much;
lines.simpleEnglish: If you value all people, but you do not let any one person become so important that they control you completely.

difficultWords.definition: Matter to you or have importance in your eyes.
No one matters so much that they overpower your judgment or self-respect.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that mature love and respect need balance: people matter, but they should not become your master.
lines.modernExample: You respect your parents, friends, partner, and teachers, but you do not let any of them force you into something wrong.
lines.connection: This expands the previous line. It explains how friends and enemies should be held: with value, but not with slavery.
lines.application: Respect everyone, but keep your conscience and judgment stronger than people-pleasing.
lines.checkQuestion: How can all people matter to you, but none too much?

lines.original: If you can fill the unforgiving minute
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can use every strict, passing minute fully instead of wasting it.

difficultWords.definition: Use completely or make full.
Time that does not stop, wait, or give back what you waste.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that maturity includes respect for time and disciplined effort.
lines.modernExample: Instead of scrolling during your study hour, you give that hour real focused work.
lines.connection: After discussing people and relationships, the poem turns to time, the final test of discipline.
lines.application: Treat time as something that will not return. Use small units of time with purpose.
lines.checkQuestion: Why is the minute called unforgiving?

lines.original: With sixty seconds’ worth of distance run,
lines.simpleEnglish: By giving a full minute of real effort, like running as far as you can for all sixty seconds.

difficultWords.definition: A complete minute used fully.
The amount of progress made through effort, like running forward.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that time should be filled with action, not empty intention.
lines.modernExample: During a 25-minute study session, you actually study for 25 minutes instead of pretending while checking your phone.
lines.connection: This completes the previous line by explaining how to fill the unforgiving minute: with full effort.
lines.application: Measure time not by how long it passes, but by how much honest effort you put into it.
lines.checkQuestion: What does it mean to fill a minute with distance run?

lines.original: Yours is the Earth and everything that’s in it,
lines.simpleEnglish: If you can do all these things, you will be capable of handling life and its opportunities.

difficultWords.definition: A poetic way of saying you will be ready to possess, face, or master life.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that the person who develops these qualities gains a kind of mastery over life.
lines.modernExample: A person with calmness, patience, honesty, resilience, humility, courage, and discipline can survive many environments and succeed in many fields.
lines.connection: This line gives the reward after all the conditions listed throughout the poem.
lines.application: Do not chase the reward first. Build the qualities that make you worthy of handling the reward.
lines.checkQuestion: What kind of person becomes ready for the Earth and everything in it?

lines.original: And—which is more—you’ll be a Man, my son!
lines.simpleEnglish: And even more important than gaining the world, you will become a truly mature, strong, responsible person.

difficultWords.definition: Something even more important than what was just said.
Here it means a mature, responsible, self-controlled adult, not merely a male person.

lines.proof: The writer is proving that character is greater than worldly success. The real achievement is becoming mature in soul and conduct.
lines.modernExample: Someone may become rich or famous, but if they are impatient, arrogant, dishonest, and weak under pressure, they have not become truly mature.
lines.connection: This is the final conclusion of the poem. After all the tests, the reward is not only external success but inner maturity.
lines.application: Judge growth by character: calmness, honesty, humility, courage, patience, resilience, and disciplined use of time.
lines.checkQuestion: According to the final line, what is more important than gaining the world?

books.title: The Enchiridion
books.author: Epictetus
books.label: Written 135 A.C.E.; translated by Elizabeth Carter

sections.title: 1. Some Things Are in Our Control
sections.summary: The foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
sections.mainIdea: The foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
sections.modernTakeaway: Do not let external events control your inner life; train your judgment and actions instead.

lines.original: Some things are in our control and others not.
lines.simpleEnglish: Epictetus is saying that you must first separate what you can control from what you cannot control.

difficultWords.definition: Power to decide or direct something.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, in daily life, pause before reacting and ask what part is truly yours to control.
lines.connection: This begins the section and introduces its main idea.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: Things in our control are opinion, pursuit, desire, aversion, and, in a word, whatever are our own actions.
lines.simpleEnglish: Epictetus is saying that you must first separate what you can control from what you cannot control.

difficultWords.definition: Power to decide or direct something.
A judgment or belief about something.
Going after something you want.
Wanting to get something.
Wanting to avoid something.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, wanting a phone, food, praise, or entertainment becomes dangerous when your peace depends on getting it.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: Things not in our control are body, property, reputation, command, and, in one word, whatever are not our own actions.
lines.simpleEnglish: Epictetus is saying that you must first separate what you can control from what you cannot control.

difficultWords.definition: Power to decide or direct something.
Things a person owns.
What other people think or say about you.
Authority, rank, or power over others.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, if people criticize you online, you cannot fully control their opinion, but you can control whether you act with dignity.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: The things in our control are by nature free, unrestrained, unhindered; but those not in our control are weak, slavish, restrained, belonging to others.
lines.simpleEnglish: Epictetus is saying that you must first separate what you can control from what you cannot control.

difficultWords.definition: Power to decide or direct something.
Not held back.
Not blocked.
Not free; dependent on someone or something else.
Held back or limited.
The proper reality or character of something.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, in daily life, pause before reacting and ask what part is truly yours to control.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: Remember, then, that if you suppose that things which are slavish by nature are also free, and that what belongs to others is your own, then you will be hindered.
lines.simpleEnglish: Keep this idea actively in your mind and use it when real life tests you.

difficultWords.definition: Not free; dependent on someone or something else.
The proper reality or character of something.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, in daily life, pause before reacting and ask what part is truly yours to control.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: You will lament, you will be disturbed, and you will find fault both with gods and men.
lines.simpleEnglish: This line gives a direct rule for how you should train your judgment and behavior.

difficultWords.definition: To express sadness or grief.
Mentally upset or unsettled.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, in daily life, pause before reacting and ask what part is truly yours to control.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: But if you suppose that only to be your own which is your own, and what belongs to others such as it really is, then no one will ever compel you or restrain you.
lines.simpleEnglish: This corrects or limits the previous idea by showing the other side of the argument.

difficultWords.definition: To force someone.

lines.proof: The writer is trying to prove that the foundation of freedom is separating what belongs to your own choice from what belongs to externals.
lines.modernExample: For example, in daily life, pause before reacting and ask what part is truly yours to control.
lines.connection: This continues from the previous line by adding a reason, example, warning, or practical consequence.
lines.application: Use the same thinking whenever something pulls your emotions: separate fact from judgment, identify what is in your control, and choose the action that protects your character.
lines.checkQuestion: What part of this line is in your control?

lines.original: Further, you will find fault with no one or accuse no one.

lines.original:
"Conduct me, Jove, and you, 0 Destiny,
Wherever your decrees have fixed my station."
Cleanthes
"I follow cheerfully; and, did I not,
Wicked and wretched, I must follow still
Whoever yields properly to Fate, is deemed
Wise among men, and knows the laws of heaven."
Euripides, Frag. 965
And this third:
"0 Crito, if it thus pleases the gods, thus let it be. Anytus and Melitus may kill me indeed, but hurt me they cannot."
Plato's Crito and Apology

books.title: Apology
books.author: Plato
books.label: Socrates' Defense; translated by Benjamin Jowett

sections.title: Socrates' Defense
sections.summary: Socrates answers the old rumors and formal charges against him. He explains that he speaks plainly, that his reputation came from questioning those who claimed wisdom, and that he values truth, virtue, and obedience to the divine mission over safety.
sections.mainIdea: Socrates argues that the accusations against him come from rumors, wounded pride, and misunderstanding, not from truth.
sections.modernTakeaway: Do not judge by reputation, crowd opinion, or polished speech; examine whether the accusation is actually true.

lines.original: How you have felt, O men of Athens, at hearing the speeches of my accusers, I cannot tell; but I know that their persuasive words almost made me forget who I was - such was the effect of them; and yet they have hardly spoken a word of truth.
lines.simpleEnglish: Socrates is talking about the people who accuse him and explaining why their claims are false or dangerous.

difficultWords.definition: People who claim someone has done wrong.
Able to make people believe or agree.

lines.proof: He is trying to defend Socrates by showing that truth, justice, and moral integrity matter more than public opinion or fear.
lines.modernExample: A person is judged online because old rumors spread about them before anyone checks the facts.
lines.connection: This is the first line of this section.
lines.application: When listening to any argument, separate truth from style. Ask what evidence supports the claim.
lines.checkQuestion: What is Socrates trying to make the judges understand in this line?

lines.original: But many as their falsehoods were, there was one of them which quite amazed me; - I mean when they told you to be upon your guard, and not to let yourselves be deceived by the force of my eloquence.
lines.simpleEnglish: Socrates is making a point in his defense and asking the audience to judge by reason, truth, and justice rather than appearances.

difficultWords.definition: Powerful and attractive speaking.

lines.proof: He is trying to defend Socrates by showing that truth, justice, and moral integrity matter more than public opinion or fear.
